heading: Sixto timing graph now has a source-native two-branch law packet with a localized cyan anomaly lane
text:
type: observation
tags: [qa, sixto, timing-graph, law-packet, anomaly]
body: Built `sixto_graph_two_branch_law_packet.json` and `build_sixto_graph_two_branch_law_packet.py` to promote the Sixto timing graph from an interpolation packet into a source-native branch law statement. The active packet is now: shared negative branch family `shared_left_template` for all four traces; shared positive branch family `shared_right_template` for `curve_graph_light_green`, `curve_graph_green`, and `curve_graph_blue`; explicit anomaly lane `cyan_positive_anomaly` for `curve_graph_cyan`. Both shared families use the green curve as medoid. The cyan anomaly is no longer vague: it is represented as a residual on the shared positive template with support window `t in [0.4, 0.5]`, support count `3`, and minimum delta `-0.699542` at `t=0.45`. Honest verdict: the timing graph no longer needs to be described as four separate curves or one forced global law. The stable source-native packet is a two-branch family plus one localized cyan positive-branch notch anomaly.
